test_cases.intent: What is the specialist copay on the Oak Premier PPO plan?

test_cases.name: hsa_eligible_plans
test_cases.description: Find all plans that allow opening an HSA account
test_cases.intent: Which Oak plans let me open an HSA?

expected_output.response: Only 1 Oak Health plan is HSA-eligible: the Oak HDHP (OAK-HDHP-2025). As a high-deductible health plan, it qualifies for a Health Savings Account. Note that FSA cannot be combined with an HSA, so the HDHP does not offer FSA. The other 6 plans are not HSA-eligible.

test_cases.name: compare_premier_ppo_vs_hdhp
test_cases.description: Compare Premier PPO and HDHP on key dimensions
test_cases.intent: Show me the key differences between the Premier PPO and the HDHP plans.

expected_output.response:
Key differences:

Oak Premier PPO ($650/mo): embedded $500 deductible, $4,000 OOP max, $50 specialist copay, out-of-network at 50% coinsurance, no HSA.
Oak HDHP ($280/mo): aggregate $1,600 individual / $3,200 family deductible, $5,000 OOP max, out-of-network at 40%, HSA-eligible, $0 telehealth pre-deductible.

The HDHP saves $370/mo but uses an aggregate deductible — meaning no family member gets coverage until the entire family pool is met.

test_cases.name: current_plan_vs_hdhp_cost_comparison
test_cases.description: Compare member's current enrolled plan cost structure against the HDHP catalog plan
test_cases.intent: How does my current plan's cost structure compare to the HDHP? Which is cheaper for me?

expected_output.response: Your current plan is a PPO with an embedded deductible. The Oak HDHP costs $280/mo (vs your current premium), has a higher $5,000 individual OOP max, and uses an aggregate family deductible of $3,200. If you are a low utilizer, the HDHP saves money on premiums and allows HSA contributions. If you have regular medical costs, your current PPO's lower OOP max and embedded deductible protect you better.

test_cases.name: member_mri_benefit_search
test_cases.description: Look up what the member's current plan covers for an MRI using the benefit search
test_cases.intent: What does my plan cover for an MRI?

expected_output.response: Under your current plan, MRI (imaging) is covered in-network at 20% coinsurance after your deductible is met. Out-of-network MRI is covered at a higher coinsurance rate after the out-of-network deductible.

test_cases.name: classic_hmo_provider_and_constraints
test_cases.description: Find providers in the Classic HMO network and explain the referral and OON constraints
test_cases.intent: If I switched to the Classic HMO, would I find a cardiologist near me in NY? Also, what's the difference with that plan?

expected_output.response: The Classic HMO uses the ACME network. Cardiologists are available in the ACME network in NY (11211). However, the Classic HMO has two important constraints: (1) it requires a PCP referral before you can see any specialist, and (2) it has zero out-of-network coverage — any care outside the ACME network (except emergencies) is 100% your responsibility.

test_cases.name: deductible_accumulator_mid_year_switch
test_cases.description: Assess the financial impact of switching plans mid-year given existing deductible progress
test_cases.intent: I've already spent some money toward my deductible this year. Would it make financial sense to switch to the Premier PPO now?

expected_output.response: You have met $250 of your current $1,000 in-network individual deductible, with $750 remaining. If you switch to the Oak Premier PPO (individual deductible: $500), your accumulated $250 resets to $0 on the new plan. You would need to spend $500 again before coverage kicks in — a net loss of the $250 you already paid. A mid-year switch is unlikely to be cost-effective unless your remaining medical needs for the year are minimal.

test_cases.name: annual_cost_projection_all_plans
test_cases.description: Project worst-case annual cost (premiums + full OOP max) across all 7 plans for a heavy healthcare user
test_cases.intent: I'm a heavy healthcare user. Which Oak Health plan gives me the lowest total annual cost — premiums plus what I'd pay out of pocket in a worst-case year?

expected_output.response:
For a worst-case year (hitting the OOP maximum), total annual cost = (monthly premium × 12) + individual OOP max:

- HDHP: $3,360 + $5,000 = $8,360 (aggregate deductible; HSA savings can reduce this further)
- Classic HMO: $4,200 + $5,000 = $9,200 (referrals required; no OON)
- EPO Select: $4,800 + $6,000 = $10,800 (no OON)
- Value HMO: $2,400 + $8,700 = $11,100 (cheapest premium but highest OOP exposure; aggregate deductible)
- POS Flex: $6,000 + $5,500 = $11,500
- Premier PPO: $7,800 + $4,000 = $11,800 (highest premium but lowest OOP max)
- Standard PPO: $5,400 + $7,000 = $12,400

For a heavy user who will hit the OOP max, the HDHP has the lowest total. However, its aggregate deductible is risky for families — Premier PPO is the safest if you have dependents.

test_cases.name: family_plan_switch_heavy_users
test_cases.description: Recommend whether a heavy-use family should stay on PPO or switch to Value HMO, using accumulator data and catalog comparison
test_cases.intent: I have a family of 4 and we've been heavy healthcare users this year. Should I stay on my current PPO or switch to the Value HMO for next year?

expected_output.response:
For a family of 4 that uses healthcare heavily, switching to the Value HMO carries significant risk:

1. Deductible structure: Your current PPO has an embedded deductible — each family member individually triggers coverage after their $500 deductible, regardless of what others have spent. The Value HMO uses an aggregate $4,000 deductible: no family member gets post-deductible coverage until all four members' claims collectively reach $4,000. One member's high-cost care could consume the entire pool.

2. OOP max: The Value HMO individual OOP max is $8,700 (the ACA statutory maximum). Your current PPO has a lower ceiling.

3. Jan 1 reset: All deductible progress resets at the start of the new plan year regardless of which plan you choose.

Recommendation: For heavy healthcare users with dependents, the embedded PPO deductible structure is substantially safer. The $450/mo Value HMO savings become a liability in a high-utilization year.

test_cases.name: oon_travel_plan_switch
test_cases.description: Recommend the single best Oak plan for a member who frequently needs care out of state
test_cases.intent: I'm reviewing Oak plans during open enrollment. I travel frequently to California and sometimes need care there. Which Oak plan has the lowest out-of-network coinsurance rate, and what is the annual premium?

expected_output.response:
For frequent care in California, you need a plan with out-of-network (OON) coverage. Three Oak plans have no OON: Classic HMO, EPO Select, and Value HMO — any non-emergency care on those plans in California is 100% your cost.

Best option: Oak POS Flex
- OON coinsurance: 30% — lowest of any Oak plan (Standard PPO and HDHP both charge 40%; Premier PPO charges 50%)
- Self-referral allowed OON — no referral needed before seeing a specialist in California
- Annual premium: $6,000 ($500/month × 12)
- Worst-case annual cost (premium + individual in-network OOP max): $6,000 + $5,500 = $11,500

Switch to the POS Flex for the lowest OON coinsurance and full self-referral flexibility when you need care out of state.

test_cases.name: hdhp_aggregate_family_deductible_trap
test_cases.description: Explain the aggregate vs embedded deductible structural trap for a family comparing HDHP to PPO, grounded in the member's actual accumulator data
test_cases.intent: The HDHP family deductible is $3,200 — that sounds lower than our PPO. Is it actually a trap?

expected_output.response:
Yes — this is a classic aggregate deductible trap.

HDHP ($3,200 aggregate): All 4 family members' expenses pool toward one shared $3,200 limit. No individual gets post-deductible coverage until the pool is fully exhausted. If one child has a $3,200 hospitalization, they alone absorb the entire family deductible — leaving all other members unshielded.

Premier PPO ($1,000 embedded): Each family member has their own $500 individual deductible cap. Once one person spends $500 in-network, their coverage kicks in regardless of what others have spent. The family aggregate ceiling is $1,000.

Your current accumulators show each family member's deductible tracked independently — that is the embedded structure protecting each person separately.

For families with any member who has predictable medical costs (chronic condition, child with asthma, etc.), the embedded PPO is far safer — even though $3,200 nominally sounds lower than $1,000.

test_cases.name: family_claims_oop_analysis_and_plan_recommendation
test_cases.description: My family has had a lot of healthcare visits this year. Tell me: (1) the total out-of-pocket responsibility my entire family (me and my two children) incurred across all claims this year, broken down per member; (2) how that compares to our family in-network deductible and OOP accumulator progress; (3) which Oak catalog plan minimizes worst-case annual cost for a family, and would switching to the Oak HDHP actually save us money given the deductible structure; and (4) find primary care doctors near me who accept new patients for Tom's follow-up care.
test_cases.intent: My family has had a lot of healthcare visits this year. Tell me: (1) the total out-of-pocket responsibility my entire family (me and my two children) incurred across all claims this year, broken down per member; (2) how that compares to our family in-network deductible and OOP accumulator progress; (3) would switching to the Oak HDHP actually save us money given its deductible structure; and (4) find primary care doctors near me who accept new patients for Tom's follow-up care.

expected_output.response:
Family 2025 member responsibility from claims:

Current family in-network accumulator progress (official plan-year tracking):
Family Deductible: $700 met of $3,000 limit
Family Out-of-Pocket: $1,200 met of $6,000 limit

Oak HDHP analysis for a family:
The HDHP has the lowest premium ($820/mo family), but uses an AGGREGATE $3,200 family deductible. This means no family member receives post-deductible coverage until ALL family members' expenses collectively reach $3,200 — unlike your current PPO's embedded structure where each member's deductible is tracked independently. With 3 active family members each filing multiple claims, the aggregate structure is a significant financial risk.
